    20→namespace Nat
    21→
    22→/-- The greatest prime divisor of a natural number `n > 1`.
    23→
    24→Takes the junk value `0` for `n = 0` and `n = 1`.
    25→
    26→TODO Consider redefining this using the computable implementation:
    27→`n.primeFactorsList.getLastI`. -/
    28→noncomputable def maxPrimeFac (n : ℕ) : ℕ := sSup {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n}
    29→
    30→@[simp]
    31→lemma maxPrimeFac_zero :
    32→    maxPrimeFac 0 = 0 := by
    33→  simpa [maxPrimeFac] using Set.Infinite.Nat.sSup_eq_zero infinite_setOf_prime
    34→
    35→@[simp]
    36→lemma maxPrimeFac_one :
    37→    maxPrimeFac 1 = 0 := by
    38→  suffices {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p = 1} = ∅ by simp [maxPrimeFac, this]
    39→  aesop
    40→
    41→lemma prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt (n : ℕ) (h : 1 < n) :
    42→    Prime (maxPrimeFac n) := by
    43→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    44→  have hs₀ : s.Nonempty := by
    45→    simp only [Set.Nonempty, Set.mem_setOf_eq, ← ne_one_iff_exists_prime_dvd, hs]
    46→    omega
    47→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    48→    use n
    49→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    50→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt h) hp
    51→  exact (Nat.sSup_mem hs₀ hs₁).1
    52→
    53→lemma maxPrimeFac_eq_of_dvd_of_le
    54→    (n p : ℕ) (hn : 0 < n) (hp : p.Prime) (h_dvd : p ∣ n) (h_le : maxPrimeFac n ≤ p) :
    55→    maxPrimeFac n = p := by
    56→  refine le_antisymm h_le ?_
    57→  replace hn : 1 < n := Nat.lt_of_lt_of_le hp.one_lt (Nat.le_of_dvd hn h_dvd)
    58→  set s := {p : ℕ | p.Prime ∧ p ∣ n} with hs
    59→  have hs₁ : BddAbove s := by
    60→    use n
    61→    simp only [hs, mem_upperBounds, Set.mem_setOf_eq, and_imp]
    62→    exact fun p _ hp ↦ Nat.le_of_dvd (zero_lt_of_lt hn) hp
    63→  exact ConditionallyCompleteLattice.le_csSup s p hs₁ ⟨hp, h_dvd⟩
    64→
    65→@[simp]
    66→lemma one_lt_maxPrimeFac_iff (n : ℕ) :
    67→    1 < maxPrimeFac n ↔ 1 < n := by
    68→  rcases lt_trichotomy n 1 with hn | rfl | hn
    69→  · simp only [lt_one_iff] at hn
    70→    simp [hn]
    71→  · simp
    72→  · simpa [hn] using (prime_maxPrimeFac_of_one_lt n hn).one_lt
    73→

    18→import Mathlib.Data.Real.Basic
    19→import Mathlib.Order.Filter.AtTopBot.Defs
    20→
    21→/-!
    22→# Beurling primes
    23→
    24→*References:*
    25→ - [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beurling_zeta_function)
    26→ - [Beurling Zeta Functions, Generalised Primes, and Fractal Membranes](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0410270)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open Filter
    30→
    31→/-- A sequence of real numbers `1 < a 0 < a 1 < ...` is called a set of Beurling prime numbers if
    32→it tends to infinity. -/
    33→noncomputable def IsBeurlingPrimes (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    34→  1 < a 0 ∧ StrictMono a ∧ Tendsto a atTop atTop
    35→
    36→/-- The set of Beurling integers are numbers of the form `∏ i, (a i) ^ (k i)`, where `k` has
    37→finite support. -/
    38→def BeurlingInteger (a : ℕ → ℝ) : Set ℝ :=
    39→  letI g : (ℕ →₀ ℕ) → ℝ := fun k => k.prod (fun x y => (a x) ^ y)
    40→  .range g
    41→
    42→/-- Every element of the sequence `a` is a Beurling integer. -/
    43→lemma generator_mem_beurling (a : ℕ → ℝ) (i : ℕ) : a i ∈ BeurlingInteger a :=
    44→  ⟨Finsupp.single i 1, by aesop⟩
    45→
    46→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under multiplication. -/
    47→lemma mul_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x y : ℝ} (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) (hy : y ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :
    48→    x * y ∈ BeurlingInteger a := by
    49→  obtain ⟨k, rfl⟩ := hx
    50→  obtain ⟨l, rfl⟩ := hy
    51→  exact ⟨k + l, by simp [Finsupp.prod_add_index', pow_add]⟩
    52→
    53→/-- The set of Beurling integers is closed under taking powers. -/
    54→lemma pow_mem_beurling {a : ℕ → ℝ} {x : ℝ} (k : ℕ) (hx : x ∈ BeurlingInteger a) :
    55→    x ^ k ∈ BeurlingInteger a := by
    56→  induction k with
    57→  | zero => exact ⟨0, by norm_num⟩
    58→  | succ k ih => simpa [pow_succ'] using mul_mem_beurling hx ih
    59→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Primitive elements of a set with respect to divisibility
    21→
    22→An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n` is in `S`.
    23→
    24→This concept appears naturally when studying sequences closed under "divisor inheritance" -
    25→if every member `n` of a sequence has the property that `m * n` is also in the sequence
    26→for coprime `m`, then the primitive elements generate the entire sequence.
    27→
    28→## Main definitions
    29→
    30→* `Set.IsPrimitive`: A natural number `n` is primitive in `S` if `n ∈ S` and
    31→  `n.properDivisors` is disjoint from `S`.
    32→* `Set.primitives`: The set of primitive elements of `S`.
    33→
    34→## Main results
    35→
    36→* `Set.IsPrimitive.mem`: A primitive element is in the set.
    37→* `Set.IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor`: Proper divisors of a primitive element are not
    38→  in the set.
    39→* `Set.one_isPrimitive_iff`: `1` is primitive in `S` iff `1 ∈ S`.
    40→* `Set.prime_isPrimitive_iff`: A prime `p` is primitive in `S` iff `p ∈ S` and `1 ∉ S`.
    41→-/
    42→
    43→namespace Set
    44→
    45→/-- An element `n` is *primitive* in a set `S ⊆ ℕ` if `n ∈ S` and no proper divisor of `n`
    46→is in `S`. -/
    47→def IsPrimitive (S : Set ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    48→  n ∈ S ∧ Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S
    49→
    50→/-- The set of primitive elements of `S`. -/
    51→def primitives (S : Set ℕ) : Set ℕ :=
    52→  {n | S.IsPrimitive n}
    53→
    54→theorem IsPrimitive.mem {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) : n ∈ S := h.1
    55→
    56→theorem IsPrimitive.disjoint_properDivisors {S : Set ℕ} {n : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n) :
    57→    Disjoint (n.properDivisors : Set ℕ) S := h.2
    58→
    59→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_properDivisor {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    60→    (hd : d ∈ n.properDivisors) : d ∉ S :=
    61→  Set.disjoint_left.mp h.2 hd
    62→
    63→theorem IsPrimitive.not_mem_of_dvd_of_lt {S : Set ℕ} {n d : ℕ} (h : S.IsPrimitive n)
    64→    (hdvd : d ∣ n) (hlt : d < n) : d ∉ S :=
    65→  h.not_mem_of_properDivisor (Nat.mem_properDivisors.mpr ⟨hdvd, hlt⟩)

    74→  constructor <;> intro ⟨hmem, hdiv⟩

    19→/-!
    20→# Wall-Sun-Sun primes
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [Wikipedia, Lucas_sequence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas_sequence)
    24→- [Wikipedia, Wall–Sun–Sun prime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_prime)
    25→- [Wikipedia, Wieferich prime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas%E2%80%93Wieferich_prime)
    26→-/
    27→
    28→open scoped NumberTheorySymbols
    29→
    30→/--
    31→**The Lucas sequence of the first kind**
    32→$U_0(P, Q) = 0$, $U_1(P, Q)=1$, $U_{n+2}(P, Q)=PU_{n+1}(P, Q)-QU_n(P, Q)$
    33→-/
    34→def LucasSequence.U (P Q : ℤ) : ℕ → ℤ
    35→  | 0 => 0
    36→  | 1 => 1
    37→  | n + 2 => P * LucasSequence.U P Q (n + 1) - Q * LucasSequence.U P Q n
    38→
    39→/--
    40→**The Lucas sequence of the second kind**
    41→$V_0(P, Q) = 0$, $V_1(P, Q)=P$, $V_{n+2}(P, Q)=PV_{n+1}(P, Q)-QV_n(P, Q)$
    42→-/
    43→def LucasSequence.V (P Q : ℤ) : ℕ → ℤ
    44→  | 0 => 2
    45→  | 1 => P
    46→  | n + 2 => P * LucasSequence.V P Q (n + 1) - Q * LucasSequence.V P Q n
    47→
    48→/--
    49→**The Lucas numbers**
    50→$L_0 = 2$, $L_1=1$, $L_{n+2} = L_{n+1}+L_n$
    51→-/
    52→def lucasNumber : ℕ → ℤ := LucasSequence.V 1 (-1)
    53→
    54→/--
    55→**Wall–Sun–Sun prime**
    56→A prime $p$ is a Wall–Sun–Sun prime if and only if $L_p \equiv 1 \pmod{p^2}$, where $L_p$ is
    57→the $p$-th Lucas number.
    58→-/
    59→structure IsWallSunSunPrime (p : ℕ) : Prop where
    60→  prime : p.Prime
    61→  lucasNumber_modeq : lucasNumber p ≡ 1 [ZMOD (p ^ 2)]
    62→
    63→/--
    64→**Lucas–Wieferich prime**
    65→A Lucas–Wieferich prime associated with $(a,b)$ is an odd prime $p$, not dividing $a^2 - 4b$, such
    66→that $U_{p-\varepsilon}(a,b) \equiv 0 \pmod{p^2}$ where $U(a,b)$ is the Lucas sequence of the first
    67→kind and $\varepsilon$ is the Legendre symbol $\left({\tfrac {a^{2}-4b}{p}}\right)$.
    68→
    69→See first paragraph of
    70→https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_prime#Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_primes_with_discriminant_D
    71→for the condition that $p$ is odd and coprime to the discriminant
    72→-/
    73→structure IsLucasWieferichPrime (a b p : ℕ) : Prop where
    74→  prime : p.Prime
    75→  odd : Odd p
    76→  not_dvd : ¬(p : ℤ) ∣ a ^ 2 - 4 * b
    77→  modeq : LucasSequence.U a b (p - J(a^2 - 4*b | p)).toNat ≡ 0 [ZMOD (p^2)]
    78→

    22→/-- `n.squarefreePart` is the unique `a₀ : ℕ` such that `a₀` is squarefree and `n = a₀ * b ^ 2`,
    23→for some `b : ℕ`. At `0` this property is not well defined because `b` must be `0` and `a₀` can be
    24→any squarefree number; we give the junk value `1` at `n = 0` following the convention that the
    25→squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/
    26→def squarefreePart (n : ℕ) : ℕ := n.factorization.prod fun (p e : ℕ) ↦ p ^ (e % 2)
    27→
    28→example : squarefreePart 2 = 2 := by
    29→  decide +native
    30→
    31→example : squarefreePart 5 = 5 := by
    32→  decide +native
    33→
    34→example : squarefreePart 4 = 1 := by
    35→  decide +native
    36→
    37→example : squarefreePart 8 = 2 := by
    38→  decide +native
    39→
    40→example : squarefreePart 16 = 1 := by
    41→  decide +native
    42→
    43→example : squarefreePart 24 = 6 := by
    44→  decide +native

    46→theorem squarefreePart_ne_zero (n : ℕ) : n.squarefreePart ≠ 0 := by
    47→  simp [squarefreePart]
    48→
    49→theorem squarefreePart_zero : squarefreePart 0 = 1 := by
    50→  simp [squarefreePart]
    51→
    52→/-- If `n` is squarefree, then its squarefree part is itself. -/
    53→theorem squarefreePart_of_squarefree {n : ℕ} (hn : Squarefree n) :
    54→    squarefreePart n = n := by
    55→  nth_rw 2 [← n.factorization_prod_pow_eq_self fun _ ↦ by simp_all]
    56→  simp only [squarefreePart, Finsupp.prod, support_factorization]
    57→  exact Finset.prod_congr rfl fun p hp ↦ by
    58→    rw [factorization_eq_one_of_squarefree hn (mem_primeFactors.1 hp).1 (mem_primeFactors.1 hp).2.1]
    59→
    60→/-- The squarefree part of any square is `1`. -/
    61→theorem squarefreePart_of_isSquare {n : ℕ} (hn : IsSquare n) :
    62→    squarefreePart n = 1 := by
    63→  rcases eq_or_ne n 0 with (rfl | h₀); exact squarefreePart_zero
    64→  obtain ⟨r, rfl⟩ := hn
    65→  rw [ne_eq, mul_eq_zero, or_self] at h₀
    66→  rw [squarefreePart, Finsupp.prod, support_factorization,
    67→    Finset.prod_congr rfl fun p hp ↦ by rw [r.factorization_mul h₀ h₀]]
    68→  simp [← two_mul]
    69→
    70→/-- If $n = \prod_p p^{e_p}$ is the prime factorization of $n$, then $\prod_p p^{e_p \pmod{2}}$
    71→is the prime factorization of the squarefree part of $n$. -/
    72→theorem squarefreePart_factorization (n : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) :
    73→    n.squarefreePart.factorization p = n.factorization p % 2 := by
    74→  rw [squarefreePart, prod_factorization_eq_prod_primeFactors,
    75→    n.prod_primeFactors_factorization_apply hp (f := fun p n ↦ n.factorization p % 2)
    76→      (fun _ _ h ↦ by simp [factorization_eq_zero_of_not_dvd h]) (by simp)]
    77→
    78→theorem Prime.squarefree {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) : Squarefree p := Irreducible.squarefree hp
    79→
    80→theorem squarefree_squarefreePart (n : ℕ) : Squarefree n.squarefreePart := by
    81→  refine Nat.squarefree_iff_factorization_le_one n.squarefreePart_ne_zero |>.2 fun p ↦ ?_
    82→  by_cases hp : p.Prime
    83→  · linarith [n.squarefreePart_factorization hp, Nat.mod_lt (n.factorization p) two_pos]
    84→  · linarith [factorization_eq_zero_of_not_prime n.squarefreePart hp]
    85→

    88→  exact Nat.factorization_prime_le_iff_dvd n.squarefreePart_ne_zero h₀ |>.1 fun p hp ↦
    89→    squarefreePart_factorization _ hp ▸ Nat.mod_le _ _

    91→/-- The square part is the value of `b ^ 2` in the squarefree decomposition of `n = a₀ * b ^ 2`. -/

    97→theorem squarefreePart_mul_squarePart (n : ℕ) : n.squarefreePart * n.squarePart = n :=

    25→the $k$-th power $p^k$ also divides $n$.

    52→for every prime divisor $p$, $p^2$ divides $n$.

1→/-

    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→import FormalConjectures.Wikipedia.LegendreConjecture
    19→
    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 375
    22→
    23→*References:*
    24→ - [erdosproblems.com/375](https://www.erdosproblems.com/375)
    25→ - [ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    26→    theory. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    27→ - [RST75] Ramachandra, K. and Shorey, T. N. and Tijdeman, R., On Grimm's problem relating to
    28→    factorisation of a block of consecutive integers. J. Reine Angew. Math. (1975), 109-124.
    29→ -
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Set Filter Topology Asymptotics
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos375
    35→
    36→/-- This is a proposition saying that for any `n ≥ 1` and any `k`, if `n + 1, ..., n + k` are all
    37→composite, then there are distinct primes `p₁, ... pₖ` such that `pᵢ ∣ n + i` for all `1 ≤ i ≤ k`.
    38→-/
    39→def Erdos375Prop : Prop := ∀ n ≥ 1, ∀ k, (∀ i < k, ¬ (n + i + 1).Prime) →
    40→    ∃ p : Fin k → ℕ, p.Injective ∧ ∀ i, (p i).Prime ∧ p i ∣ n + i + 1
    41→
    42→/-- Is `Erdos375Prop` true? -/
    43→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    44→theorem erdos_375 : answer(sorry) ↔ Erdos375Prop := by
    45→  sorry
    46→
    47→/-- If `Erdos375Prop` is true, then `(n + 1).nth Prime - n.nth Prime < (n.nth Prime) ^ (1 / 2 - c)`
    48→for some `c > 0`. -/
    49→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    50→theorem erdos_375.bounded_gap : Erdos375Prop →
    51→    ∃ c > 0, ∀ᶠ n in atTop, (n + 1).nth Nat.Prime - n.nth Nat.Prime
    52→    < (n.nth Nat.Prime : ℝ) ^ (1 / (2 : ℝ) - c) := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→/-- In particular, if `Erdos375Prop` is true, then Legendre's conjecture is asymptotically true. -/
    56→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    57→theorem erdos_375.legendre : Erdos375Prop →
    58→    (∀ᶠ n in atTop, ∃ p ∈ Set.Ioo (n ^ 2) ((n + 1) ^ 2), Nat.Prime p) :=
    59→  fun hp => LegendreConjecture.bounded_gap_legendre (erdos_375.bounded_gap hp)
    60→
    61→/-- It is easy to see that for any `n ≥ 1` and `k ≤ 2`, if `n + 1, ..., n + k` are all composite,
    62→then there are distinct primes `p₁, ... pₖ` such that `pᵢ ∣ n + i` for all `1 ≤ i ≤ k`. -/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    64→theorem erdos_375.le_two : ∀ n ≥ 1, ∀ k ≤ 2, (∀ i < k, ¬ (n + i + 1).Prime) →
    65→    ∃ p : Fin k → ℕ, p.Injective ∧ ∀ i, (p i).Prime ∧ p i ∣ n + i + 1 := by
    66→  intro n hn k hk
    67→  interval_cases k <;> intro h
    68→  · simp_all; intro; grind
    69→  · choose! p hp using (n + 1).exists_prime_and_dvd (by linarith)
    70→    exact ⟨fun x => p, fun x => by grind, fun i => by simpa using hp⟩
    71→  · choose! p hp using (fun i : Fin 2 => (n + i + 1).exists_prime_and_dvd (by linarith))
    72→    refine ⟨p, fun x y hxy => ?_, hp⟩
    73→    by_contra! hr
    74→    wlog hq : x < y
    75→    · exact this n hn k hk h p hp y x hxy.symm hr.symm (by grind)
    76→    · have hy : y = x + 1 := by grind
    77→      have := hy ▸ Nat.dvd_sub (hp y).2 (hxy ▸ (hp x).2)
    78→      have := (hp 1).1
    79→      simp_all [Nat.not_prime_one]
    80→
    81→/-- There exists a constant `c > 0` such that for all `n`, if
    82→`k < c * (log n / (log (log n))) ^ 3 → (∀ i < k, ¬ (n + i + 1).Prime)`, then
    83→there are distinct primes `p₁, ... pₖ` such that `pᵢ ∣ n + i` for all `1 ≤ i ≤ k`. This is proved
    84→in [RST75]. There is no need to only consider sufficiently large `n` because one can always take
    85→`c` small enough so that `k < c * (log n / (log (log n))) ^ 3` implies that `k = 0` until `n` is
    86→large. -/
    87→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    88→theorem erdos_375.log : ∃ c > 0, ∀ n k : ℕ,
    89→    k < c * (Real.log n / (Real.log (Real.log n))) ^ 3 → (∀ i < k, ¬ (n + i + 1).Prime) →
    90→    ∃ p : Fin k → ℕ, p.Injective ∧ ∀ i, (p i).Prime ∧ p i ∣ n + i + 1 := by
    91→  sorry
    92→
    93→end Erdos375
    94→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 220
    21→
    22→Is it true that the sum of squared gaps between consecutive totatives of $n$ is
    23→$O(n^2 / \varphi(n))$?
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/220](https://www.erdosproblems.com/220)
    26→
    27→[MoVa86] Montgomery, H.L. and Vaughan, R.C., _On the distribution of reduced residues_, 1986.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open Finset
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos220
    33→
    34→/-- The sum of squared consecutive gaps in a sorted list of natural numbers. -/
    35→def sumSquaredGaps : List ℕ → ℕ
    36→  | [] => 0
    37→  | [_] => 0
    38→  | a :: b :: rest => (b - a) ^ 2 + sumSquaredGaps (b :: rest)
    39→
    40→/-- The totatives of $n$ listed in increasing order: all $m$ with $1 \le m < n$
    41→and $\gcd(m, n) = 1$. -/
    42→def sortedTotatives (n : ℕ) : List ℕ :=
    43→  ((Finset.range n).filter (fun m => 0 < m ∧ Nat.Coprime m n)).sort (· ≤ ·)
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Erdős Problem 220 [Er40, Er55c, Er57, Er61, Er65b, Er73, Er79, ErGr80, Er81k, Er85c]:
    47→
    48→Let $n \ge 1$ and $A = \{a_1 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(n)}\} = \{1 \le m < n : \gcd(m,n) = 1\}$ be the
    49→totatives of $n$ listed in increasing order. Is it true that
    50→$$\sum_{1 \le k < \varphi(n)} (a_{k+1} - a_k)^2 \ll n^2 / \varphi(n)?$$
    51→
    52→The answer is yes, as proved by Montgomery and Vaughan [MoVa86], who showed
    53→the more general result that for any $\gamma \ge 1$,
    54→$$\sum_{1 \le k < \varphi(n)} (a_{k+1} - a_k)^\gamma \ll n^\gamma / \varphi(n)^{\gamma-1}.$$
    55→-/
    56→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    57→theorem erdos_220 :
    58→    answer(True) ↔
    59→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    60→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n →
    61→      (sumSquaredGaps (sortedTotatives n) : ℝ) ≤ C * (n : ℝ) ^ 2 / (Nat.totient n : ℝ) := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→end Erdos220
    65→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 141
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [erdosproblems.com/141](https://www.erdosproblems.com/141)
    24→- [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primes_in_arithmetic_progression#Consecutive_primes_in_arithmetic_progression)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→namespace Erdos141
    28→
    29→/--
    30→The predicate that a set `s` consists of `l` consecutive primes (possibly infinite).
    31→This predicate does not assert a specific value for the first term.
    32→-/
    33→def Set.IsPrimeProgressionOfLength (s : Set ℕ) (l : ℕ∞) : Prop :=
    34→    ∃ a, ENat.card s = l ∧ s = {(a + n).nth Nat.Prime | (n : ℕ) (_ : n < l)}
    35→
    36→open Nat Erdos141
    37→
    38→/--
    39→The first three odd primes are an example of three consecutive primes.
    40→-/
    41→@[category test, AMS 5 11]
    42→theorem first_three_odd_primes : ({3, 5, 7} : Set ℕ).IsPrimeProgressionOfLength 3 := by
    43→  use 1
    44→  constructor
    45→  · aesop
    46→  · norm_num [exists_lt_succ_right, or_assoc, eq_comm, Set.insert_def,
    47→    show (2).nth Nat.Prime = 5 from nth_count prime_five,
    48→    show (3).nth Nat.Prime = 7 from Nat.nth_count (by decide : (7).Prime)]
    49→
    50→/--
    51→The predicate that a set `s` is both an arithmetic progression of length `l` and a progression
    52→of `l` consecutive primes.
    53→-/
    54→def Set.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength (s : Set ℕ) (l : ℕ) :=
    55→   s.IsAPOfLength l ∧ s.IsPrimeProgressionOfLength l
    56→
    57→/--
    58→There are 3 consecutive primes in arithmetic progression.
    59→-/
    60→@[category test, AMS 5 11]
    61→theorem exists_three_consecutive_primes_in_ap : ∃ (s : Set ℕ), s.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength 3 := by
    62→  use {3, 5, 7}
    63→  constructor
    64→  · use 3, 2
    65→    unfold Set.IsAPOfLengthWith
    66→    constructor
    67→    · aesop
    68→    · norm_num [exists_lt_succ_right, or_assoc, eq_comm, Set.insert_def]
    69→  · exact first_three_odd_primes
    70→
    71→/--
    72→Let $k≥3$. Are there $k$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?
    73→-/
    74→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    75→theorem erdos_141 : answer(sorry) ↔
    76→    ∀ k ≥ 3, ∃ (s : Set ℕ), s.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength k := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→/--
    80→The existence of such progressions has been verified for $k≤10$.
    81→-/
    82→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    83→theorem erdos_141.variants.first_cases :
    84→    (∀ k ≥ 3, k ≤ 10 → ∃ (s : Set ℕ), s.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength k) := by
    85→  sorry
    86→
    87→/--
    88→Are there $11$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?
    89→-/
    90→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
    91→theorem erdos_141.variants.eleven : answer(sorry) ↔
    92→    ∃ (s : Set ℕ), s.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength 11 := by
    93→  sorry
    94→
    95→/--
    96→The set of arithmetic progressions of consecutive primes of length $k$.
    97→-/
    98→def consecutivePrimeArithmeticProgressions (k : ℕ) : Set (Set ℕ) :=
    99→  {s | s.IsAPAndPrimeProgressionOfLength k}
   100→
   101→/--
   102→It is open, even for $k=3$, whether there are infinitely many such progressions.
   103→-/
   104→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
   105→theorem erdos_141.variants.infinite_three : answer(sorry) ↔
   106→    (consecutivePrimeArithmeticProgressions 3).Infinite := by
   107→  sorry
   108→
   109→/--
   110→Fix a $k \geq 3$. Is it true that there are infinitely many arithmetic prime progressions of length $k$?
   111→-/
   112→@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
   113→theorem erdos_141.variants.infinite_general_case : answer(sorry) ↔
   114→    ∀ k ≥ 3, (consecutivePrimeArithmeticProgressions k).Infinite := by
   115→  sorry
   116→
   117→end Erdos141

    14→limitations under the License.

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1138
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1138](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1138)
    23→
    24→Is the expected asymptotic formula for the number of primes in short intervals of length
    25→proportional to the maximal prime gap valid? Specifically, for $x/2 < y < x$ and $C > 1$,
    26→is $\pi(y + Cd) - \pi(y) \sim Cd / \log y$, where $d$ is the maximal prime gap below $x$?
    27→
    28→[Va99] Vardi, I., 1.3.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos1138
    32→
    33→/-- The maximal gap between consecutive primes up to $x$.
    34→For each index $k$ with the $k$-th prime (0-indexed) less than $x$,
    35→compute the gap to the next prime and take the maximum.
    36→Returns $0$ when there are no primes less than $x$. -/
    37→noncomputable def maxPrimeGap (x : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    38→  (Finset.range (Nat.primeCounting' x)).sup
    39→    (fun k => Nat.nth Nat.Prime (k + 1) - Nat.nth Nat.Prime k)
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 1138 [Va99, 1.3]:
    43→
    44→Let $x/2 < y < x$ and $C > 1$. If $d = \max_{p_n < x} (p_{n+1} - p_n)$ is the maximal
    45→prime gap below $x$, where $p_n$ denotes the $n$-th prime, then is it true that
    46→$$\pi(y + Cd) - \pi(y) \sim Cd / \log y?$$
    47→
    48→In other words, the expected asymptotic formula for the number of primes in the
    49→interval $[y, y + Cd]$ holds. This is a combination of two well-studied problems:
    50→finding the minimum $h$ for which $\pi(y + h) - \pi(y) \sim h / \log y$, and understanding
    51→the maximal prime gap $d$. The conjectured size of $d$ is $\approx (\log x)^2$, which is far
    52→below the $h$ obtainable even assuming the Riemann hypothesis.
    53→-/
    54→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    55→theorem erdos_1138 : answer(sorry) ↔
    56→    ∀ (C : ℝ), 1 < C → ∀ (ε : ℝ), 0 < ε →
    57→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ x : ℕ, N ≤ x →
    58→        ∀ y : ℕ, x < 2 * y → y < x →
    59→          |((Nat.primeCounting (y + ⌊C * (maxPrimeGap x : ℝ)⌋₊) : ℝ) -
    60→            (Nat.primeCounting y : ℝ)) /
    61→            (C * (maxPrimeGap x : ℝ) / Real.log (y : ℝ)) - 1| < ε := by
    62→  sorry
    63→
    64→end Erdos1138
    65→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→open scoped Finset
    20→
    21→/-!
    22→# Erdős Problem 56
    23→
    24→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/56](https://www.erdosproblems.com/56)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→namespace Erdos56
    28→
    29→/--
    30→Say a set of natural numbers is `k`-weakly divisible if any `k+1` elements
    31→of `A` are not relatively prime.
    32→-/
    33→def WeaklyDivisible (k : ℕ) (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    34→    ∀ s ∈ A.powersetCard (k + 1), ¬ Set.Pairwise s Nat.Coprime
    35→
    36→@[category API, AMS 11]
    37→lemma weaklyDivisible_empty (k : ℕ): WeaklyDivisible k {} := by
    38→  simp [WeaklyDivisible]
    39→
    40→/-- A singleton is `k`-weakly divisble if `k ≠ 0`. -/
    41→@[category API, AMS 11]
    42→lemma weaklyDivisible_singleton {k : ℕ} (hk : k ≠ 0) (l : ℕ) : WeaklyDivisible k {l} := by
    43→  simp [WeaklyDivisible, hk]
    44→
    45→/-- No non-empty set is `1`-weakly divisible. -/
    46→@[category API, AMS 11]
    47→lemma not_weaklyDivisible_zero {A : _} (h : A.Nonempty) : ¬WeaklyDivisible 0 A := by
    48→  simpa [WeaklyDivisible] using ⟨{_}, by simpa using h.choose_spec⟩
    49→
    50→@[category API, AMS 11]
    51→lemma empty_iff_weaklyDivisible_zero {A : _} : WeaklyDivisible 0 A ↔ A = ∅ :=
    52→  ⟨fun h ↦ Finset.not_nonempty_iff_eq_empty.1 <| mt not_weaklyDivisible_zero (not_not.2 h),
    53→    fun h ↦ h ▸ weaklyDivisible_empty _⟩
    54→
    55→/--
    56→`MaxWeaklyDivisible N k` is the size of the largest k-weakly divisible subset of `{1,..., N}`
    57→-/
    58→noncomputable def MaxWeaklyDivisible (N : ℕ) (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    59→  sSup {#A | (A : Finset ℕ) (_ : A ⊆ Finset.Icc 1 N) (_ : WeaklyDivisible k A)}
    60→
    61→@[category test, AMS 11]
    62→theorem maxWeaklyDivisible_zero : ∀ k : ℕ, MaxWeaklyDivisible 0 k = 0 := by
    63→  intro k
    64→  simp [MaxWeaklyDivisible, Nat.sSup_def]
    65→
    66→@[category test, AMS 11]
    67→theorem maxWeaklyDivisible_one {k : ℕ} (hk : k ≠ 0) : MaxWeaklyDivisible 1 k = 1 := by
    68→  have : {x | ∃ A, WeaklyDivisible k A ∧ (A = ∅ ∨ A = {1}) ∧ #A = x} = {0, 1} := by
    69→    refine Set.ext fun _ => ⟨fun _ => by aesop, ?_⟩
    70→    rintro ⟨_, _⟩
    71→    · simpa using weaklyDivisible_empty k
    72→    · exact ⟨{1}, by simp_all [weaklyDivisible_singleton hk 1]⟩
    73→  simp_all [MaxWeaklyDivisible]
    74→
    75→@[category test, AMS 11]
    76→theorem maxWeaklyDivisible_zero_k (N : ℕ) : MaxWeaklyDivisible N 0 = 0 := by
    77→  simp [empty_iff_weaklyDivisible_zero, MaxWeaklyDivisible]
    78→
    79→/--
    80→`FirstPrimesMultiples N k` is the set of numbers in `{1,..., N}` that are
    81→a multiple of one of the first `k` primes.
    82→-/
    83→noncomputable def FirstPrimesMultiples (N k : ℕ) : Finset ℕ :=
    84→    (Finset.Icc 1 N).filter fun i => ∃ j < k, (j.nth Nat.Prime ∣ i)
    85→
    86→@[category test, AMS 11]
    87→theorem firstPrimesMultiples_one_card_zero (k : ℕ) : (FirstPrimesMultiples 1 k).card = 0 := by
    88→  simp [FirstPrimesMultiples, Finset.filter_singleton]
    89→  intro n h
    90→  by_contra hprime
    91→  have : Nat.Prime 1 := by
    92→    convert Nat.prime_nth_prime n
    93→    exact hprime.symm
    94→  tauto
    95→
    96→@[category test, AMS 11]
    97→theorem firstPrimesMultiples_zero_k_card_zero (N : ℕ) : (FirstPrimesMultiples N 0).card = 0 := by
    98→  simp [FirstPrimesMultiples]
    99→
   100→/--
   101→An example of a `k`-weakly divisible set is the subset of `{1, ..., N}`
   102→containing the multiples of the first `k` primes.
   103→-/
   104→@[category API, AMS 11]
   105→lemma weaklyDivisible_firstPrimesMultiples (N k : ℕ) (hN : 1 ≤ N) :
   106→    WeaklyDivisible k (FirstPrimesMultiples N k) := by
   107→  sorry
   108→
   109→/--
   110→Suppose $A \subseteq \{1,\dots,N\}$ is such that there are no $k+1$ elements of $A$ which are
   111→relatively prime. An example is the set of all multiples of the first $k$ primes.
   112→Is this the largest such set?  To avoid trivial counterexamples, we must insist that $N$ be at
   113→least the $k$th prime.
   114→-/
   115→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   116→theorem erdos_56 : (∀ᵉ (k > 0) (N ≥ (k-1).nth Nat.Prime),
   117→    (MaxWeaklyDivisible N k = (FirstPrimesMultiples N k).card)) ↔
   118→    answer(False) := by
   119→  sorry
   120→
   121→end Erdos56
   122→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 235
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/235](https://www.erdosproblems.com/235)
    23→
    24→Let $N_k = p_1 \cdots p_k$ be the $k$-th primorial and let $a_1 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(N_k)}$
    25→be the integers less than $N_k$ coprime to $N_k$. Erdős conjectured that the fraction of
    26→consecutive gaps $a_{i+1} - a_i \le c \cdot N_k / \varphi(N_k)$ tends to a limit that is a
    27→continuous function of $c$. This was proved by Hooley.
    28→
    29→[Er55c] Erdős, P., _Some problems on number theory_ (1955).
    30→
    31→[Ho65] Hooley, C., _On the difference of consecutive numbers prime to n_, Acta Arith. 8 (1963),
    32→343-347.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Filter Nat Classical
    36→
    37→open scoped BigOperators
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos235
    40→
    41→/-- The $k$-th primorial: product of the first $k$ primes ($p_1 \cdot p_2 \cdots p_k$). -/
    42→noncomputable def primorial (k : ℕ) : ℕ := ∏ i ∈ Finset.range k, nth Nat.Prime i
    43→
    44→/-- The sorted list of natural numbers less than $n$ that are coprime to $n$. -/
    45→def sortedCoprimes (n : ℕ) : List ℕ :=
    46→  ((Finset.range n).filter (fun a => Nat.Coprime a n)).sort (· ≤ ·)
    47→
    48→/-- Count consecutive gaps $\le$ threshold in a sorted list of naturals. -/
    49→noncomputable def countSmallGaps : List ℕ → ℝ → ℕ
    50→  | [], _ => 0
    51→  | [_], _ => 0
    52→  | a :: b :: rest, threshold =>
    53→    (if ((b : ℝ) - (a : ℝ)) ≤ threshold then 1 else 0) + countSmallGaps (b :: rest) threshold
    54→
    55→/-- The fraction of consecutive gaps among reduced residues $\bmod N_k$
    56→that are at most $c \cdot N_k / \varphi(N_k)$. -/
    57→noncomputable def gapFraction (k : ℕ) (c : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    58→  let Nk := primorial k
    59→  let residues := sortedCoprimes Nk
    60→  let threshold := c * (Nk : ℝ) / (Nat.totient Nk : ℝ)
    61→  (countSmallGaps residues threshold : ℝ) / (Nat.totient Nk : ℝ)
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Erdős Problem 235 [Er55c] (proved by Hooley [Ho65]):
    65→
    66→Let $N_k = 2 \cdot 3 \cdots p_k$ (the $k$-th primorial) and let
    67→$a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(N_k)}$ be the integers less than $N_k$ coprime to $N_k$.
    68→Then for any $c \ge 0$, the limit
    69→$$
    70→\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\#\{i : a_i - a_{i-1} \le c \cdot N_k / \varphi(N_k)\}}{\varphi(N_k)}
    71→$$
    72→exists and defines a continuous function of $c$.
    73→
    74→Hooley proved that the limiting distribution is exponential: $f(c) = 1 - e^{-c}$.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    77→theorem erdos_235 :
    78→    ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, Continuous f ∧
    79→      ∀ c : ℝ, 0 ≤ c →
    80→        Tendsto (fun k => gapFraction k c) atTop (nhds (f c)) := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→end Erdos235
    84→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 795
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/795](https://www.erdosproblems.com/795)
    23→
    24→Let $g(n)$ be the maximal size of $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, n\}$ such that the subset products
    25→$\prod_{a \in S} a$ are distinct for all $S \subseteq A$. Erdős proved that
    26→$g(n) \leq \pi(n) + O(\sqrt{n} / \log n)$. This upper bound is essentially best possible,
    27→since one could take $A$ to be all primes and squares of primes.
    28→
    29→[Ra25] Raghavan, S., _On the Erdős distinct subset products problem_, 2025.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Finset BigOperators
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos795
    35→
    36→/-- A finset of natural numbers has distinct subset products if for all subsets
    37→$S, T \subseteq A$, $\prod_{a \in S} a = \prod_{a \in T} a$ implies $S = T$. -/
    38→def HasDistinctSubsetProducts (A : Finset ℕ) : Prop :=
    39→  ∀ S T, S ⊆ A → T ⊆ A → (∏ i ∈ S, i) = (∏ i ∈ T, i) → S = T
    40→
    41→/-- The prime counting function $\pi(n)$: the number of primes $\leq n$. -/
    42→def primeCounting (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    43→  ((Finset.range (n + 1)).filter Nat.Prime).card
    44→
    45→/-- $g(n)$: the maximal cardinality of $A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, n\}$ with distinct subset
    46→products. -/
    47→noncomputable def maxDistinctProductSetSize (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    48→  sSup {k : ℕ | ∃ A : Finset ℕ, (∀ a ∈ A, 1 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ n) ∧
    49→    HasDistinctSubsetProducts A ∧ A.card = k}
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 795: $g(n) \leq \pi(n) + \pi(\lfloor\sqrt{n}\rfloor) + o(\sqrt{n} / \log n)$.
    53→
    54→Formulated as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N_0$ such that for all $n \geq N_0$,
    55→$g(n) \leq \pi(n) + \pi(\lfloor\sqrt{n}\rfloor) + \varepsilon \cdot \sqrt{n} / \log n$.
    56→
    57→This was solved by Raghavan [Ra25], who proved
    58→$g(n) \leq \pi(n) + \pi(\lfloor\sqrt{n}\rfloor) + O(n^{5/12 + o(1)})$.
    59→-/
    60→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    61→theorem erdos_795 :
    62→    answer(True) ↔
    63→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    64→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    65→      (maxDistinctProductSetSize n : ℝ) ≤
    66→        (primeCounting n : ℝ) + (primeCounting (Nat.sqrt n) : ℝ) +
    67→        ε * (Nat.sqrt n : ℝ) / Real.log (n : ℝ) := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→end Erdos795
    71→
